title: Dog Ate Moldy Garbage: Veterinary Triage & Emergency Guide
meta_desc: If your dog ate moldy garbage, they may be at risk for mycotoxin poisoning. This is a high-urgency emergency. Learn the signs and next steps here.
content_html:
TL;DR: Tremorgenic mycotoxin poisoning from moldy food is a life-threatening veterinary emergency that requires immediate medical attention to prevent seizures and neurological damage.

What is tremorgenic mycotoxin poisoning in dogs and how does it affect them?

When a dog eats moldy garbage, they are likely ingesting tremorgenic mycotoxins. These are toxic compounds produced by fungi growing on decomposing organic matter, such as old bread, cheese, or walnuts. Unlike a simple upset stomach, these toxins specifically target the central nervous system, leading to rapid neurological decline.

Is tremorgenic mycotoxin poisoning a veterinary emergency for my dog?

  • Urgency Level: High. This is a critical veterinary emergency as mycotoxin poisoning can progress with alarming speed.
  • If left untreated, the toxins can cause severe muscle tremors, life-threatening seizures, and dangerously high body temperatures (hyperthermia).
  • You should contact your local emergency vet or a pet poison control hotline immediately after ingestion.

How can taking a photo of the moldy food help the veterinarian treat my dog?

  • If it is safe to do so, take a clear photo of the moldy item your dog consumed before heading to the clinic.
  • Different types of mold produce different toxins; for example, moldy dairy or nuts can be particularly potent.
  • A photo allows the veterinary team to better identify the source and tailor their decontamination and treatment protocols to the specific risks involved.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Dogs are commonly involved in garbage ingestion due to their indiscriminate eating habits. The warm, moist environment of the stomach serves as an efficient incubator for the replication of yeast within dough, if present in the garbage. Expanding dough mass can cause gastric distention, leading to vascular compromise of the gastric wall, similar to gastric dilatation/volvulus, and potentially respiratory compromise. Yeast fermentation products include ethanol, which is absorbed, resulting in inebriation and metabolic acidosis. Early clinical signs may include unproductive attempts at emesis and abdominal distention. As ethanol intoxication develops, ataxia and disorientation may occur, eventually progressing to profound CNS depression, weakness, recumbency, coma, hypothermia, or seizures. Death is usually due to the alcohol's effects, although the potential for dough to trigger gastric dilatation/volvulus should not be overlooked in susceptible breeds. A presumptive diagnosis can be based on a history of exposure and clinical signs; blood ethanol levels are consistently increased in bread dough toxicosis. Differential diagnoses include gastric dilatation/volvulus, foreign body obstruction, ethylene glycol toxicosis, and ingestion of other CNS depressants. With recent ingestions in asymptomatic animals, emesis may be attempted, although the glutinous nature of bread dough may make removal via emesis difficult. Time after ingestion and observed clinical signs are important for determining treatment and prognosis, especially in the case of possible mushroom ingestion. If mushroom ingestion is suspected, identification of the mushroom species is ideal, while treatment focuses on decontamination and intensive supportive care.

Chapter: Emergency, Toxicology, Gastroenterology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 2965)

faqs.question: What symptoms should I look for?
faqs.answer: Early signs include vomiting and agitation, which can quickly progress to staggering, muscle tremors, and full-body seizures.

faqs.question: How long does it take for mycotoxins to affect a dog?
faqs.answer: Symptoms typically appear within 30 minutes to three hours after the dog has eaten the moldy food.

faqs.question: Should I induce vomiting at home?
faqs.answer: Do not induce vomiting unless specifically instructed by a veterinarian, as this can be dangerous if the dog is already showing neurological signs or if they could inhale the vomit.
